speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is March 27, 1996. We are conducting an interview with Mr. Marvin Zborowski. The interviewer is Anita White. We're conducting the interview in Jamaica Estates, USA. The language of the interview is English. My name is Anita White-- W-H-I-T-E. Today's date is March 27, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with the survivor Mr. Marvin Zborowski. The interview is conducted in Jamaica Estates, Queens, USA. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Marvin Zborowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-B-O-R-O-W-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My middle name, [? Henry. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Zarki, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-A-R-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on June 4, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us, please, next to what large town in Poland is your city, Zarki, and how large was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The town of Zarki was population 8,000, which 50% of the population were Jews. The town was located 30 kilometers-- in other words, 20 miles-- from the town of Czestochowa, which was famous for the Black Madonna. And to the south, we were close to town of Sosnowiec, which was a very industrial city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zisel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was in the leather business, and we had tanneries working for us, having the finished product back to our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have an older brother and a younger sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us, please, something about-- whatever you remember from your childhood about the city itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: The city itself was a quite vibrant city. Consisted of all various affiliation and organizations. I was too young to join, even too young to join the scouts. I attended school. I do remember my home quite vividly. We lived quite comfortable. We had a house, our own house, which is still in existence today. Consisted of three rooms plus a kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any household staff?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did have a household cleaning lady and a woman taking care of the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me something about religion in your house. What affiliation did you have? And tell us something about your holidays and life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our family was considered as religious. We were devoted to the religion. My father was attending services twice a day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were going every Sabbath-- the children, with my father, we went to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did family get together for holidays? And which holidays did you celebrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: We celebrated all the holidays. And especially, I would outline the holidays of Pesach, which is Passover, where the family got together. And I also had my grandparents in the same town, so I used to go, as a grandson, for Passover. During the Tabernacle holidays of Sukkot, we were blessed that our home was quite-- considered, in those days, as a luxury. We had a built-in tabernacle in the house, which is being called the sukkah. And we even have guests in our home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you use at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say, most of the-- most of the time, I would say Polish, but as well, Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us something about your schoolings. Which schools did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: I attend cheder at the early age of four, five. Then, of course, public school, which was quite obligatory to attend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience-- while you were in your public school, did you experience any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do-- I did experience some kind of uneasiness, but I would say that it was not that the children did understand what means 'anti-Semitism.' If they came with a certain hatred or discomfort, it was only because probably, or most likely, they were-- they were taught at home, or being told at home. Same thing goes on our behalf. Coming from a Jewish home, I don't think so that I was free to associate with the Gentile children. And the same, probably-- the Gentile children could not associate with the Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the years before the war, in the town itself, are-- are you aware if there was any anti-Semitism in the town itself, in the town proper, against-- you said half of the population was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do remember vividly one incident, which was a very tragic one. And this was in a summer month where school-- the public school-- was closed, and I attended cheder, which is the Hebrew school. In the town arrived, in the marketplace, or to say, the center of the town-- a bus arrived. In those days, a bus-- it was for me, as a child, a novice, to run to the bus and see it. I mean, it's a bus, luxurious bus. I didn't even had an opportunity to run closely to the bus, whereby 30 or 40 young men jumped out of the bus, just destroying Jewish property. Just practically beating up Jewish people, which was a very unusual thing for me and will always remember in my memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, who were these young people? Did they come from the outside of town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was told later on that this was an organization. They were wearing some kind of a uniform. And that they belonged to a certain extreme organization which were the anti-Jewish. I would not say 'anti-Semitism.' They were anti-Jewish. I did not understand the full meaning of the word 'anti-Semitism.'

speaker: interviewer
text: In the years before the war, in this town where you said your father and you were so comfortable, did you have any sense or feeling of danger from Germany, that things were changing? And did you or anybody in town consider leaving Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did-- always, my father-- my late father was always speaking about the danger, obviously since the time of Hitler coming to power. And being that our town was so close to the German border, we were always worried about it. But this was not the question that we were aware. We were aware. But still and all, not every house had a radio. Not every house had a telephone. And, in turn, we were limited even with the newspapers, so to say. Even though that the government of Poland, as I read, later years, between 1920 and 1939, it was most of the time was not a free country. It was a dictatorship under the Polish government, where no actual publications or papers could be printed that their own way of thinking. Besides, I would say people had-- even if they were aware of anti-Semitism, there was no way where to run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there no-- no immigration to Palestine at that time at all, from the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: From later time that I was told, a later date, that to Palestine you could only go if you were either belonging to an organization that's one of the scouts organization, or from the extreme left of the halutzim, which means 'the pioneers.' Or the ones, let's say, like my father, having a family-- he could have also emigrated by showing that he has means to live in Israel. In other words, a certain amount of money, which will give him the preference of going to Israel and settling, cultivate the land of Israel. At those days, of course, not Israel-- it was Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about the cultural or the social life of the town, the kehilla and the organizations which might have helped the Jewish population? Do you remember any of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do remember quite well. The town was a social-oriented and cultural-oriented town. Starting with all the organizations, from extreme left to the extreme right, were in the town. Children had places where to go. We especially, being at a young age, we were very comfortable at home. We were considered one of the, so to say, wealthy people. Having a home, living in-- quite comfortable, having to eat quite comfortable. And even, we had-- every day we had, from dawn, we had a higher school of learning in Jewish studies, whereby a yeshiva boy, which means a boy which attended the higher studies, Jewish studies, came to our home and everyday, to have a lunch, which at those day was considered as a dinner.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, you learned charity early from your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father was very charitable. And besides being charitable, he always believed of helping people [SPEAKING YIDDISH] which, in other words, it has the meaning of helping people to establish themselves. Not just giving outright as well, but giving people to be able to sustain and live a better life.

speaker: interviewer
text: The loan society, gemilut hasadim.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the first day of the war? Do you remember when it happened, and where were you? Describe to us the first day of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do remember. It was September 1, on a Friday. We did not even realize. People were just preparing for the Sabbath. School year just started. It was very festive. It was before the High Holidays. Nobody was-- even though with the news that were coming that Germany is really arming themself, we still couldn't believe that it is so close. Still and all, September 1, the war broke out. On September 2, which was on a Sabbath, the town was practically completely destroyed by bombs.

speaker: interviewer
text: How so?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly how many planes, but the city was destroyed by bombs-- destructive and firebombs. The town was practically in flames.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there-- were there people who were killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a lot of the casualties at that day. The most interesting part is why they had to bomb this town, which was no industry. But five kilometers, meaning two and a half miles, was the heaviest industry of the country of Poland-- was not touched. Meaning, in other words, they knew that this belongs to them, just to create the havoc of people [? of ?] the scariness of war. They bombed the surrounding areas, not the industrial areas.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: On Sunday, the second day, so to say, of the war-- the first, second Sunday-- they just simply went through, like-- like, just driving through the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans came.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans came in, and they occupied the town, of course, creating various scary shootings, plundering everything possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the beginning. This seemingly-- as I was, at that time, being a boy of 11, not realizing what means 'SS,' and not realizing what means 'Wehrmacht,' or what means 'Germans,' to me, a motorcycle or the cars were the interesting part to go and see. Seemingly, I could not go and see because, just at the sight, they were just killing. So everybody was in hiding. Not the question of Jewish population or-- the population of the town, period. Then, on Monday-- this is in the history, written many books that I found after the war-- they were considering this the Bloody Monday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: The reason the Bloody Monday was that they came into town, seemingly the black uniforms-- which later on, we found out that this is the SS 'Sturmtruppen,' so to say. They were killing-- just merciless, merciless, wherever, just-- just killing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they killing Jews only?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews and Gentile, but most of them Jews that I could say. Because the Jews lived in an area in that small town close, so to say, within the vicinity. So the center of that town was Jewish, so this whole center was destroyed. But a lot of killings, a lot of people. And this was-- I was told later on, also, that this was not in our town. It's the whole area of the Czestochowa area, all towns went through the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when did they establish any government in your city? When did they start running the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say that there was no official government. One thing I have to say-- even though that I am Jewish, but at the same time, I'm born in Poland-- there was no Polish government which collaborated with the Germans in comparison of later days and years. That the Slovakian army or government had collaborated, Hungary had a government that collaborated, Norway had a government that collaborated, France--

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- but what did they do in Zarki, in your town? Did they establish a Jewish communal government, or did they have a German government, or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: At a later date, I would say that they let the people run the, so to say, the city council, which consisted mostly of Gentiles. And the Jews again now established their own committee, which was also approved by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did life change once the Germans came in? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Life changed from day one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they ask you to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyday, there were new-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [? [SPEAKING POLISH] ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Orders.

speaker: interviewee
text: --new orders--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ordinances.

speaker: interviewee
text: --ordinances how to make life more difficult, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us, what did they ask?

speaker: interviewee
text: The life of difficulty was, first of all, depriving the people to exercise their profession. Most of the people were businesspeople. So therefore, they could not do any commerce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why they took away the businesses? What did they do about the businesspeople?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the businesses were confiscated. If it was larger enterprises, they put in a [? treuhander, ?] which I would say, it's a governing person which was watching over your business. Our business was confiscated. They came with trucks, load it, and everything. I do even remember that there was not the question that they were plundering, and robbing, and taking away processions. The Germans, they took everything. In 1938, we finished our public school. It was a new school that new benches, that I don't think so that even today is in existence. Which I do remember distinctly, each individual student had his own bench, with high polish. This school-- all the furnitures from that school was, in the first week of the war, taking out and sent to Germany. So don't ever think that they did not know where it coming from. They were plundering, confiscating. Whatever it was movable, they took it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when they took things away from business, when they confiscated the business, did they give any remuneration? Did they give any-- any certificate that they took anything, that they confiscated--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell us about other laws, aside from confiscating business. What else did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: So this-- this is the next step, how they try to humiliate the people. In other words, by taking away the sustenance and to be able to support themselves, there were no humane ways. Then, of course, people had to risk their lives and go out of the town, bringing in just staple food like potatoes, flour, just simply to be able to sustain and live. Then, of course, came the ordinance of giving away personal possessions. Like, for instance, jewelry. So, jewelry-- people were not so eager or fast to give, so they put this in hiding, and it was easy to hide jewelry. Nobody's going to be that stupid to give away diamonds if they had diamonds, or gold if they had gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else, then, were they asking for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they starting to come out with an ordinance-- being that they were preparing for the war with Russia, they took away all the furs that the people had. Not the question of fur coats or fur lining-- anything that they had. Even if a coat had a fur collar, you had to strip your collar and deliver the collar to the Germans. It was [? Sammelstelle-- ?] that means to say, it was a collecting place. And it was written down that 'This and this family gave so many of so many.' In other words, they knew-- or they were probably denouncing that, let's say, if a family was a well-to-do family, that that family probably has more than one piece or two pieces of fur, so to say, to be delivered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was doing the denouncing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly who did, but they must have some kind of a system to find out who is well, and who is poor-- some-- I could not imagine-- even if somebody will be denouncing it, seemingly, that they were threatening that they do know. Could possibly also be that-- that they did not know, and they're just pretending that they do know. I do remember exactly an incident when it comes to this question of furs. As I said, the people-- most of the people in the town were religious people. And, being religious, you had to go twice to-- to the prayer-- for prayers to the synagogue. And at one time-- which, of course, all the synagogue was destroyed in 1939, with the exception of the big synagogue. But then, they established small synagogues in private houses, because you're not supposed to just gather together. Being that our customs is that by having 10 people you could establish a formal prayer.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a man was walking to a prayer. And he was probably-- was heavy [INAUDIBLE] this man, a sick man, and he was wearing fur pants. And the Germans spotted that he is wearing pants which are fur-lined. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was at the time when the ordinance was that all the furs has to be delivered to the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: They did not ask him to take off the-- the pants. They did not ask him-- they just told him, 'You're wearing fur pants, which they're supposed to have been delivered.' They killed him on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other things were they asking the people, the Jews, to turn in, aside from jewelry and furs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they did in a very humiliating way-- they gave ordinance, if you give so much and so much contribution of money, we will let you be free. In other words, free in a sense we will not bother you that you should give so many so many men for work, or so many, many more restrictions, or you get more rations. Don't forget, we-- all-- all the food, whatever meager part, was given to us by rations. In other words, we had little pieces of paper for rations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain that a little bit in detail.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the contributions that-- that we had to give or, so to say, to be bailed out. Let's say this town population-- let's say 4,000 Jews-- had to deliver by the date so-and-so-- has to deliver, let's say, 1 million [? zloty, ?] or 100,000, or whatever amount. By that date, that money had to be delivered.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then explain-- you said you had rations. Now explain the system how people were fed. Tell us about the rations.

speaker: interviewee
text: People were given rations, to-- to-- that they could get, let's say, a pound or two pounds of bread. I do not recall the exactly weigh. I definitely did not go to pick up my-- my portion or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did everybody in town-- was everybody in town given a small identification--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say they were giving--

speaker: interviewer
text: --card or a [INAUDIBLE] card? What were people given in order to receive the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say they were registered. I did not have any ID card whatsoever throughout my time, being, during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any-- if people did not have enough, were there any soup kitchens established at the time already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were. People were helping. My home, that I was brought up, that we always helped. Because we were fortunate to have certain reserves of money that we could help people. But I do remember at the time that 300 Jews were put into our town from-- were expelled from the north into our town. And it was on a Friday, or Thursday or Friday, and they were distributed throughout the town to people who had, let's say, larger homes. So they put in a family or whatever. And whatever they could not allocate any rooms, they were actually in the gym and the school. And they were quartered there, given some sleeping facilities that they should live there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other things were the Germans telling the Jews to bring in?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, also, I think that I overlooked one step, that all the Jews had to wear the bands with the David star. Some other areas-- let's say, in our town, was the border, the German borders. On the other side of the border, they were wearing yellow David stars with written 'Jude,' which means 'Jews.'

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have to wear in Zarki?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to wear a band with the-- with the Star of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they leave you with the radios or any of the newspaper things?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Radios had-- they were confiscated in the first days, that we should be practically cut off from-- from any news.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the community itself cope with the influx of the other people, or with the-- with the-- the poor ones in town? Or was there hygiene when people were sick? Tell us, what did the town do? I mean, community itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: The community itself was stricken with various epidemic due to the situation and the hygienic problems. Therefore, we could not be-- have any possibility to-- to-- People were dying. They were decimated. People were really without any means of living, and so on. They were really very, very poor conditions.
